title: Crooked Doghouse
summary: A storybook-crooked doghouse — the tilted, off-kilter look comes from four genuinely different truss angles, not from sloppy building.
description:
The whimsical "crooked house" look is deceptive: it takes MORE precision to build than a square one, not less. Every truss is a different, deliberate compound angle — 30°, 40°, 10° combinations cut long-point-to-long-point — and if you're sloppy instead of precise, you don't get charmingly crooked, you get a roof that doesn't close up right. Cut each truss exactly to its stated angle and the crookedness comes out looking designed, because it is.

Build all four trusses before you touch the floor panel, and dry-fit the whole roofline before you commit to gluing anything — the front and back overhang trusses have to land the same way the main trusses do, or the roof plywood won't lie flat. The curved gable shapes cut into the two large plywood panels are the other place to slow down: trace them from the plan's pattern, don't eyeball them, since those two panels are visible from the street on both sides of the house.

tools.note: A compound miter saw for the angled truss members — several of these cuts are off-square on both ends and not parallel to each other.
For breaking down the plywood panels.
For the curved gable shapes cut into the two large front/back plywood panels.

materials.name: Exterior plywood, 1/2", 4x8 sheet
materials.note: 2-1/2 sheets called for; buy 3 to allow for cutting waste on the angled panels.

materials.name: Wood glue, exterior/waterproof
materials.note: This lives outside — use a waterproof formula, not standard interior glue.

cutList.note: Top end cut at 45° off square, long-point measurement.
Top end cut at 45° off square, long-point measurement.
Both ends cut at 10° off square, NOT parallel, long-point measurement.
Curved gable shapes cut from these — trace the pattern, don't eyeball it.
Both ends 10° off square, long-point, NOT parallel.
One end of each cut at 5° off square, long-point.
One side 48" x 36", the other 48" x 32" — the crooked roofline doesn't match side to side.

steps.title: Build the four trusses
steps.body: Glue and fasten each truss with countersunk screws, driving pilot holes into the ends before assembly so the screws don't split the 2x2 stock. Every truss uses a different angle combination, so keep your cut pieces labeled and match them to the right truss.

steps.title: Mark and attach the floor
steps.body: Mark the truss locations on the floor panel. Apply glue to the tops of the panel where each truss lands, then screw down through the floor into each truss with 2" screws.

steps.title: Attach the end panels
steps.body: Attach the two end panels to the trusses as shown, using glue at every joint.

steps.title: Add the side plywood
steps.body: Attach the lower and upper side panels using 2" screws and glue.

steps.title: Trim the sides
steps.body: Trim out the sides using 1" finish nails and glue. The stated measurements run to the center of each stud, which makes for easier, more accurate nailing than measuring to a board edge.

steps.title: Trim the front and back
steps.body: Trim out the front and back panels the same way, following the angled measurements exactly — this is where a sloppy cut turns "charmingly crooked" into "actually doesn't fit."

steps.title: Trim the front door opening
steps.body: Trim out the front door opening with glue and 1" finish nails.

steps.title: Add the overhang trusses
steps.body: Attach the front and back overhang trusses, making sure to join the tops with screws and glue. If you want extra support, add ridge poles between the trusses — not strictly required, but cheap insurance on a roof this shape.

steps.title: Add the roof
steps.body: Attach the two roof plywood panels last. Because the truss angles differ, the two roof panels are different sizes — double-check you're installing the right one on the right side before you commit any fasteners.

images.alt: Whimsical crooked-style doghouse with an angled roofline and off-kilter trusses
